title: Buffer Overflow
description: Writing data beyond a buffer's boundary to overwrite adjacent memory and hijack program execution.
fullExplanation: A buffer overflow occurs when a program writes more data to a buffer than it can hold, overwriting adjacent memory. On the stack, this can overwrite the return address, redirecting execution to attacker-controlled shellcode. Stack overflows are the classic form; heap overflows corrupt heap metadata or function pointers. Modern mitigations (ASLR, DEP/NX, stack canaries) make exploitation harder but not impossible.

types.name: Stack Buffer Overflow
types.description: Overwrite return address on the stack — redirect execution to shellcode or ROP chain

types.name: Heap Buffer Overflow
types.description: Corrupt heap metadata, function pointers, or adjacent objects

types.name: Integer Overflow
types.description: Arithmetic overflow causes incorrect buffer size calculation → buffer overflow

types.name: Format String
types.description: printf(user_input) — attacker controls format string, reads/writes arbitrary memory

exploitTechniques.description: Overwrite return address with libc function (system('/bin/sh')) — no shellcode needed
Chain existing code gadgets to bypass NX/DEP — no injected code
Fill heap with shellcode + NOP sleds to increase hit probability
Access freed memory that's been reallocated with attacker data

mitigations.name: ASLR
mitigations.description: Address Space Layout Randomization — randomize memory addresses each run

mitigations.name: DEP / NX bit
mitigations.description: Data Execution Prevention — mark stack/heap as non-executable

mitigations.name: Stack Canaries
mitigations.description: Place random value before return address — detect overwrite before return

mitigations.name: Safe Languages
mitigations.description: Rust, Go, Java have bounds checking — no buffer overflows by design

keyFacts: Morris Worm (1988) used buffer overflow — first major internet worm
Heartbleed (2014): OpenSSL buffer over-read — leaked private keys
EternalBlue (2017): SMB buffer overflow — used in WannaCry ransomware
C/C++ most vulnerable — no bounds checking by default
gets(), strcpy(), sprintf() are dangerous — use fgets(), strncpy(), snprintf()
ASLR + DEP + canaries together make exploitation very difficult
ROP chains bypass DEP by reusing existing executable code
